_comment: Physical constants for the six 'other planets' (Mercury, Venus, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune) plus Earth, Mars and the Moon for orrery completeness. TRANSCRIBED from canonical sources, not a model: radius/mass/sidereal-rotation are from JPL SSD phys_par; semi-major axis + eccentricity from JPL SSD approx_pos (Table 1, 1800-2050); obliquity/mean-temperature/orbital-period from the NASA NSSDC Planetary Fact Sheet. Gas/ice giants have NO solid surface; their temperature is a 1-bar REFERENCE level (flagged per body). Negative sidereal_rotation = retrograde. See docs/PLANETS_DATA_SOURCES.md and docs/PLANETS_PHYSICS.md.

physical_parameters.provider: NASA JPL Solar System Dynamics -- Planetary Physical Parameters
physical_parameters.license: US Government (NASA/JPL/Caltech) public data; no explicit license stated

orbital_elements.provider: NASA JPL Solar System Dynamics -- Approximate Positions of the Planets (Table 1)

fact_sheet.provider: NASA NSSDCA Planetary Fact Sheet (D. R. Williams, NASA GSFC)
fact_sheet.note: nssdc.gsfc.nasa.gov host 307-redirects to nasa.gov/nssdc as of 2026-07-06 (same as the Mars fact sheet, docs/MARS_DATA_SOURCES.md). Values cross-checked against a faithful reproduction and multiple web sources; JPL fields above are the live-verified primary values and agree with NSSDC to rounding.

sources.mercury_obliquity: Margot et al. (2012): 2.042 arcmin = 0.034 deg (the NSSDC value).

Mercury.name: Mercury
Mercury.temp_kind: surface (mean; near-meaningless given the ~600 K range)

measured_temp_extremes_C.note: MESSENGER-era measured surface extremes; no atmosphere to buffer. NSSDC max 427 C / min -173 C; subsolar surface ~430 C.

Mercury.notes: No real atmosphere (tenuous exosphere). Honest dynamic signal = day/night surface-temperature extremes, the largest of any planet.

Venus.name: Venus

super_rotation.note: Cloud-top zonal winds ~100 m/s (retrograde) circle the planet in ~4 Earth days vs the 243-day solid-body rotation. Measured by Venus Express (VMC) and Akatsuki (UVI).

Venus.notes: Retrograde rotation (obliquity 177.36 deg). Honest dynamic signal = atmospheric super-rotation at the cloud tops; the surface is near-isothermal so shows no dynamics.

Jupiter.name: Jupiter
Jupiter.notes: No solid surface; rotation is System III (magnetic). Honest dynamic signal = MEASURED zonal wind profile (jets, peak ~150 m/s) + the long-lived Great Red Spot (~22 S). See zonal_winds.json.

Saturn.name: Saturn
Saturn.notes: No solid surface; bulk rotation period is uncertain (no fixed surface to track). Honest dynamic signals = MEASURED zonal winds (equatorial super-jet ~400+ m/s), the real north polar hexagon (~78 N), and the rings. See zonal_winds.json and saturn_rings.json.

Uranus.name: Uranus
Uranus.temp_kind: 1-bar reference level (no solid surface); NSSDC value carries ~2 K spread across reproductions (76-78 K)
Uranus.notes: The standout honest feature: obliquity 97.77 deg -> the planet is tipped on its side, giving ~42-year-long seasons where each pole faces the Sun for ~21 years. Retrograde rotation. Near-featureless in true color (faint bands only). Interior structure is INFERRED, not measured.

Neptune.name: Neptune
Neptune.notes: Fastest winds in the solar system (~2100 km/h ~= 580 m/s), MEASURED by Voyager 2 (1989). The Great Dark Spot is TRANSIENT (the 1989 spot was gone by 1994; new ones appear/disappear) -- must be labeled transient, not permanent. See zonal_winds.json.

Earth.name: Earth
Earth.notes: Reference body; Phase 1. a is the Earth-Moon barycenter value from JPL Table 1.

Mars.name: Mars
Mars.notes: Phase 2. Eccentricity ~5.5x Earth's drives the seasonal asymmetry (see docs/MARS_PHYSICS.md).

Moon.name: Moon
Moon.temp_kind: surface (mean; ~300 K day/night swing, see docs/MOON_PHYSICS.md)
Moon.orbit_note: Orbits Earth. semimajor_axis_au here is the mean Earth-Moon distance (384,400 km) expressed in AU, NOT a heliocentric orbit; eccentricity 0.0549 is the lunar orbit's.
Moon.notes: Phase 3. No atmosphere -> no weather; dynamic signal is the ~300 K diurnal surface-temperature swing (Diviner).
